title: SO4 Results
subtitle: Kiosk latency, IPFS cost, live cohort & factorial TPS

blocks.text: SO4 validates integrated system viability under live deployment conditions: hardware constraints, storage overhead, node failure resilience, and adversarial users. The results quantify the operational cost of the security architecture, demonstrating where the system trades convenience for integrity.

End-to-end latency was measured for both enrollment and verification transactions at the kiosk. Enrollment is a one-time process requiring fingerprint capture, facial capture, and on-chain DID anchoring. Verification is the daily attendance operation. AI inference was containerized to 2 CPU cores (Intel i5 Gen 6), the blockchain node used 1 core, and IPFS used 1 core.

blocks.caption: End-to-End Latency for Live User-Kiosk Transactions

data.title: Latency Stability & Jitter Distribution
data.subtitle: Consensus latency (ms) per workload per medium — each dot is one load level

blocks.text: At P95, enrollment takes 47.1 seconds total. The dominant contributor is the fingerprint sensor at 63.3% of total time, a hardware limitation of the commodity ADHTech GT-521F52 scanner. Face capture contributes 18.7%, AI inference 23.1%, and chain anchoring only 7.4%. The blockchain layer is the fastest component in the pipeline.

Verification at P95 takes 13.7 seconds. The dominant contributor is again biometric hardware capture at P95 of 9.9 seconds. Ledger lookup completes in under 100 ms at P95, and system compute averages 1.0 second (P95 = 4.4 s). At P50, the system supports approximately 4–5 verifications per minute.

IPFS integration cost was measured by comparing throughput with and without the storage sidecar. The penalty is dramatic at the API ingestion level (−76.5%) due to content-addressing overhead, CID computation, and LibP2P swarm propagation. However, in consensus mode, where QBFT serialization is the binding constraint, the penalty is negligible (wired) or zero (wireless). The consensus ceiling absorbs IPFS overhead entirely.

The 76.5% ingestion penalty impacts burst-mode batch enrollment most; steady-state verification is unaffected because consensus serialization is the throughput bottleneck. Mean CPU remained approximately 7% with IPFS enabled, confirming the penalty is protocol-bound rather than hardware-bound.

The live cohort of 50 users performed 200 honest verification attempts and 50 deliberate presentation attacks (spoofing) across the 4-node cluster. Zero false matches were recorded across all 50 spoofing attempts. FNMR of 31.5% is an intentional trade-off, the fingerprint gate that enforces credential-layer revocation is the same mechanism that causes genuine rejections when fingerprint quality is low. The retry mechanism carries no security penalty.

The full 2³ factorial throughput matrix reveals the performance landscape across all 8 deployment configurations. The ingestion-to-consensus ratio averages 22.2×. The ~30 TPS consensus ceiling translates to approximately 15 finalized transactions per second, or ~54,000 transactions per hour. Pre-filled versus empty ledger state showed only a −5.3% average overhead, within JVM warm-up variance and considered negligible.
